nodes.name: Famous Paintings & Sculptures
nodes.description: Iconic paintings and sculptures from antiquity to the 20th century

items.prompt: Which oil painting on poplar wood, housed in the Louvre behind bulletproof glass, measures just 30 x 21 inches and was stolen in 1911 by an Italian handyman who hid it in his apartment for two years?
items.explanation:
Leonardo da Vinci, oil on poplar panel, c. 1503–1519, Louvre, Paris
Measures only 77 x 53 cm (30 x 21 in) — far smaller than most visitors expect
Stolen in 1911 by Vincenzo Peruggia, an Italian handyman who hid it under his coat; recovered in Florence in 1913
The subject is believed to be Lisa Gherardini, wife of Florentine merchant Francesco del Giocondo
Sfumato technique creates the famous ambiguous smile — the expression appears to change depending on where you look
Francis I of France acquired it; it has been French state property since the Revolution
Survived an acid attack (1956) and a rock thrown at it (1956); now protected by bulletproof glass since 1974
Most visited artwork in the world — approximately 10 million people view it annually

items.prompt: Which swirling oil painting of a night sky over a village, now at MoMA in New York, was created in June 1889 by a Dutch Post-Impressionist while he was a patient at the Saint-Paul-de-Mausole asylum in southern France?
items.explanation:
Vincent van Gogh, oil on canvas, June 1889, Museum of Modern Art (MoMA), New York
Painted from the east-facing window of his room at the Saint-Paul-de-Mausole asylum in Saint-Rémy-de-Provence
The village depicted is partly imaginary — the church spire resembles those in the Netherlands, not Provence
Van Gogh considered it a failure and mentioned it only briefly in letters to his brother Theo
Measures 73.7 x 92.1 cm (29 x 36.3 in); relatively modest in size
Acquired by MoMA in 1941 through the Lillie P. Bliss bequest
The swirling sky may reflect turbulent atmospheric phenomena; some researchers have linked the patterns to mathematical fluid dynamics
One of the most reproduced and recognized paintings in Western art

items.prompt: Which tempera-and-crayon composition on cardboard depicting an agonized figure on a bridge against a blood-red sky was created in 1893 by a Norwegian artist, exists in four versions, and has been stolen from Oslo museums twice?
items.explanation:
Edvard Munch, 1893 (original tempera and crayon on cardboard), National Gallery, Oslo
Actually a series of four versions: two paintings and two pastels, created between 1893 and 1910
The figure is not screaming but hearing a scream — Munch wrote of sensing 'an infinite scream passing through nature'
Stolen from the National Gallery in 1994 on the opening day of the Lillehammer Winter Olympics; recovered three months later
A different version stolen by armed robbers from the Munch Museum in 2004; recovered in 2006
A pastel version sold at Sotheby's in 2012 for nearly $120 million, then a record for any artwork at auction
The blood-red sky may have been inspired by the atmospheric effects of the 1883 Krakatoa volcanic eruption
Part of Munch's larger *Frieze of Life* series exploring love, anxiety, and death

items.prompt: Which 17-foot marble statue of a biblical hero, carved from a block two other sculptors had rejected, stands in the Galleria dell'Accademia in Florence and was completed in 1504 by a 29-year-old Italian Renaissance master?
items.explanation:
Michelangelo, marble, 1501–1504, Galleria dell'Accademia, Florence
Stands 5.17 meters (17 feet) tall, carved from a single block of Carrara marble
The marble block had been abandoned by sculptors Agostino di Duccio and Antonio Rossellino as unworkable
Depicts the moment before the battle with Goliath, not after — David is tense, with veins visible in his right hand
Originally placed outside the Palazzo della Signoria as a symbol of Florentine civic power; moved indoors in 1873
An outdoor replica now stands in the original location in the Piazza della Signoria
The left hand is disproportionately large — likely deliberate, designed to be viewed from below
Survived a 1991 attack when a man struck the left foot with a hammer, breaking off a toe

items.prompt: Which bronze sculpture of a seated nude male resting his chin on one hand was originally designed as part of a larger portal called *The Gates of Hell* and exists in numerous casts worldwide, with one at the Musée Rodin in Paris?
items.explanation:
Auguste Rodin, bronze (originally modeled 1880, first large cast 1904), Musée Rodin, Paris
Originally conceived as part of *The Gates of Hell*, Rodin's monumental door inspired by Dante's *Inferno*
The figure was initially called *The Poet* and represented Dante contemplating his work
The first large-scale bronze (186 cm) was cast in 1904; over 25 authorized full-size casts exist worldwide
Depicts a muscular nude man deep in thought, right elbow on left knee — often misremembered as chin on fist of the same-side hand
Became a universal symbol of intellectual activity and philosophy
A cast outside the Pantheon in Paris was placed over Rodin's grave at his request
The Cleveland Museum of Art's cast was damaged by a pipe bomb in 1970

items.prompt: Which oil painting of a young woman in a blue-and-gold turban, often called the 'Mona Lisa of the North,' hangs in the Mauritshuis in The Hague and was painted by a Dutch Golden Age master around 1665?
alternates: The Girl with a Pearl Earring
items.explanation:
Johannes Vermeer, oil on canvas, c. 1665, Mauritshuis, The Hague
A tronie (character study) rather than a portrait — the girl's identity is unknown
The pearl is unusually large and may be tin, glass, or entirely imaginary — no pearl of that size and sheen existed in the 17th century
The turban is exotic and Oriental, unusual for Dutch genre painting of the period
Vermeer's characteristic luminous light is concentrated on the girl's face, lips, and the gleaming pearl
Virtually forgotten for centuries; rediscovered along with Vermeer's other works in the 1880s
Inspired Tracy Chevalier's 1999 novel and the 2003 film starring Scarlett Johansson
Measures just 44.5 x 39 cm (17.5 x 15.4 in) — intimate in scale

items.prompt: Which small Surrealist oil painting at MoMA depicts melting pocket watches draped over a barren landscape and was completed in 1931, reportedly inspired by the sight of Camembert cheese melting in the sun?
items.explanation:
Salvador Dalí, oil on canvas, 1931, Museum of Modern Art (MoMA), New York
Measures only 24.1 x 33 cm (9.5 x 13 in) — surprisingly small given its fame
Dalí said the melting watches were inspired by the sight of Camembert cheese melting in the sun
The barren landscape depicts the cliffs of Port Lligat in Catalonia, near Dalí's home
The soft watches symbolize the irrelevance of time in the unconscious mind — a core Surrealist theme
The fleshy form in the center is a distorted self-portrait of Dalí's own profile
Completed in under five hours, according to Dalí, while his wife Gala was at the cinema
Dalí painted a sequel, *The Disintegration of the Persistence of Memory* (1954), incorporating atomic-age imagery

items.prompt: Which painting of a stern-faced farmer holding a pitchfork beside a woman, inspired by a house with a pointed arched window in Eldon, Iowa, was exhibited at the Art Institute of Chicago in 1930, where it won a bronze medal and was purchased for $300?
items.explanation:
Grant Wood, oil on beaverboard, 1930, Art Institute of Chicago
The models were Wood's sister Nan Wood Graham and his dentist Dr. Byron McKeeby — not a married couple
Inspired by a small house with a distinctive Gothic Revival window in Eldon, Iowa (the house still stands)
Often interpreted as satire of rural Midwestern values, though Wood insisted it was affectionate tribute
Won a bronze medal at the Art Institute of Chicago's annual exhibition in 1930; the museum purchased it for $300
Became one of the most parodied images in American art and popular culture
Measures 78 x 65.3 cm (30.7 x 25.7 in); painted on beaverboard (a type of fiberboard), not canvas
Wood was part of the American Regionalism movement, along with Thomas Hart Benton and John Steuart Curry

items.prompt: Which monumental anti-war painting in grisaille (shades of grey), measuring 3.49 x 7.76 meters, was created in 1937 as a response to the bombing of a Basque town during the Spanish Civil War and hangs in the Museo Reina Sofía?
items.explanation:
Pablo Picasso, oil on canvas, 1937, Museo Reina Sofía, Madrid
Created for the Spanish Republic's pavilion at the 1937 Paris International Exposition
Depicts the Nazi German and Italian Fascist bombing of the Basque town of Guernica on April 26, 1937
Painted entirely in grisaille (black, white, and grey) to evoke newspaper photography and the horror of war
Measures 3.49 x 7.76 m (11.5 x 25.5 ft) — one of the largest paintings by any modern artist
Kept at MoMA in New York from 1939 to 1981; Picasso refused to let it return to Spain under Franco's dictatorship
Transferred to Spain in 1981, six years after Franco's death, fulfilling Picasso's wishes
When a Nazi officer saw a photo of it and asked Picasso 'Did you do this?', he reportedly replied 'No, you did'

items.prompt: Which tempera painting depicts a goddess emerging fully grown from the sea on a giant scallop shell, was painted in the mid-1480s for the Medici family, and hangs in the Uffizi Gallery in Florence?
items.explanation:
Sandro Botticelli, tempera on canvas, c. 1485, Uffizi Gallery, Florence
Commissioned by Lorenzo di Pierfrancesco de' Medici, a cousin of Lorenzo the Magnificent
Depicts Venus (Aphrodite) arriving at shore on a shell, blown by the wind god Zephyr and the nymph Chloris
The model is believed to be Simonetta Vespucci, a Florentine noblewoman renowned for her beauty
Painted on canvas rather than wood panel, unusual for the period — measures 172.5 x 278.9 cm (5.7 x 9.2 ft)
One of the first large-scale post-classical paintings of a non-biblical female nude
Nearly destroyed during Savonarola's Bonfire of the Vanities in the 1490s; survived because it was in a private villa
The pose derives from the classical Venus Pudica (modest Venus) type, such as the Medici Venus

items.prompt: Which mural depicting Jesus's final meal with his disciples, painted on the refectory wall of a Milanese convent, began deteriorating within decades because the artist used experimental tempera and oil on dry plaster instead of true fresco?
items.explanation:
Leonardo da Vinci, tempera and oil on gesso/pitch/mastic, 1495–1498, Santa Maria delle Grazie, Milan
Measures 4.6 x 8.8 m (15 x 29 ft), covering an entire wall of the convent's refectory
Leonardo used an experimental dry-wall technique instead of true fresco, allowing him to work slowly but causing rapid deterioration
Captures the moment Jesus announces 'One of you will betray me' — each apostle reacts with distinct emotion
Survived an Allied bombing in 1943 that destroyed the refectory's roof; the wall with the painting remained standing thanks to protective sandbags
Has undergone multiple restorations; the most recent (1978–1999) took 21 years
A doorway cut through the wall in 1652 removed Jesus's feet from the composition
Visitors are admitted in groups of 25 for exactly 15 minutes; reservations often book out months in advance

items.prompt: Which enormous 1642 painting of a militia company, darkened by varnish to appear like a nocturnal scene (though it actually depicts daytime), hangs in the Rijksmuseum in Amsterdam and measures 3.63 x 4.37 meters?
items.explanation:
Rembrandt van Rijn, oil on canvas, 1642, Rijksmuseum, Amsterdam
Full title: *The Militia Company of Captain Frans Banning Cocq and Lieutenant Willem van Ruytenburch*
Actually depicts a daytime scene — centuries of dark varnish and soot gave it the misleading name 'Night Watch'
Measures 3.63 x 4.37 m (11.9 x 14.3 ft), though it was trimmed on all four sides in 1715 to fit between two doors in Amsterdam's Town Hall
Revolutionary for its time: instead of a static group portrait, Rembrandt depicted the militia in dynamic motion
The central figure in black with a red sash is Captain Frans Banning Cocq; the luminous figure in gold is his lieutenant
Subjected to multiple attacks: slashed with a knife in 1911 and 1975, sprayed with acid in 1990
Underwent a major public restoration (Operation Night Watch) starting in 2019, partly conducted live for visitors behind glass

items.prompt: Which fresco showing God reaching out to touch the finger of the first man is part of a larger ceiling commission in Vatican City, painted between 1508 and 1512 while the artist worked on scaffolding 20 meters above the floor?
items.explanation:
Michelangelo, fresco, 1508–1512, ceiling of the Sistine Chapel, Vatican City
Part of a larger scheme covering the entire ceiling with scenes from Genesis — over 500 square meters of painted surface
The near-touching fingers of God and Adam have become one of the most replicated images in art history
Michelangelo painted on scaffolding he designed himself; contrary to myth, he worked standing up, not lying on his back
The shape surrounding God and the angels has been interpreted as resembling a cross-section of the human brain — possibly a deliberate anatomical reference
The entire ceiling took approximately four years; Michelangelo worked with minimal assistance
Restored between 1980 and 1994, revealing vivid colors hidden under centuries of candle soot and overpainting
Michelangelo considered himself primarily a sculptor, not a painter, and initially resisted the commission from Pope Julius II

items.prompt: Which complex 1656 painting in the Museo del Prado shows the artist at his easel with the Infanta Margarita and her attendants, while the Spanish king and queen appear reflected in a mirror on the back wall?
items.explanation:
Diego Velázquez, oil on canvas, 1656, Museo del Prado, Madrid
Measures 318 x 276 cm (10.4 x 9.1 ft) — nearly life-size figures
Central figure is the five-year-old Infanta Margarita Teresa, attended by her ladies-in-waiting (meninas)
Velázquez included himself painting at a large canvas on the left side of the composition
King Philip IV and Queen Mariana appear in a mirror on the back wall, ambiguously placed to question who is being painted
Picasso created 58 variations on the painting in 1957; Michel Foucault devoted the opening chapter of *The Order of Things* (1966) to analyzing it
Considered by many art historians to be the greatest painting in Western art for its sophisticated play with perspective, viewpoint, and reality
The title means 'The Ladies-in-Waiting' and was not used until the 19th century

items.prompt: Which series of approximately 250 oil paintings depicting a flower garden and Japanese bridge was created over 30 years by a French Impressionist at his home in Giverny, with large panels now displayed in the Musée de l'Orangerie in Paris?
items.explanation:
Claude Monet, oil on canvas, c. 1896–1926, various museums worldwide
Approximately 250 paintings in the series, produced over the last 30 years of Monet's life
Eight large panels installed in two oval rooms at the Musée de l'Orangerie in Paris, as Monet intended — offered to France the day after the WWI Armistice (November 12, 1918), though not installed until 1927
The garden at Giverny was Monet's own creation: he diverted a river to build the water garden and Japanese bridge
Late paintings became increasingly abstract as Monet's cataracts worsened, shifting his color perception toward red and brown
After cataract surgery in 1923, he destroyed several canvases he felt had been affected by his impaired vision
One painting from the series sold at Christie's in 2024 for $65.5 million
The series influenced Abstract Expressionists including Jackson Pollock and Mark Rothko

items.prompt: Which gold-leaf-laden painting shows a couple embracing on a flowered cliff, with the man's robe decorated in geometric rectangles and the woman's in circular patterns, painted 1907–1908 by the leader of the Vienna Secession?
items.explanation:
Gustav Klimt, oil and gold leaf on canvas, 1907–1908, Österreichische Galerie Belvedere, Vienna
Measures 180 x 180 cm (5.9 x 5.9 ft) — a perfect square
Painted during Klimt's 'Golden Phase,' inspired by Byzantine mosaics he saw in Ravenna, Italy
The man's robe features black-and-white rectangles; the woman's has circular floral motifs — commonly interpreted as masculine/feminine symbolism
The couple kneels at the edge of a flowered precipice, adding tension beneath the tenderness
Acquired by the Austrian state for the Belvedere in 1908 while still in progress
One of the most commercially reproduced artworks in history — appears on posters, mugs, phone cases worldwide
Not to be confused with Rodin's marble sculpture of the same name (1882)

items.prompt: Which monumental Pointillist painting (over 2 x 3 meters) depicts Parisians relaxing by the Seine on an island, composed entirely of tiny dots of color, and hangs in the Art Institute of Chicago?
items.answer: A Sunday Afternoon on the Island of La Grande Jatte
alternates: Sunday Afternoon on the Island of La Grande Jatte
items.explanation:
Georges Seurat, oil on canvas, 1884–1886, Art Institute of Chicago
Measures 207.6 x 308 cm (6.8 x 10.1 ft) — took Seurat two years to complete
Pioneering work of Pointillism (Neo-Impressionism): composed entirely of small, distinct dots of pure color that blend optically at a distance
Depicts a Sunday afternoon on La Grande Jatte, an island in the Seine near the Parisian suburb of Asnières
Seurat made over 60 preparatory studies and sketches before beginning the final canvas
Figures appear deliberately frozen and doll-like — Seurat aimed for timeless monumentality rather than Impressionist spontaneity
Inspired Stephen Sondheim's musical *Sunday in the Park with George* (1984)
Acquired by the Art Institute of Chicago in 1926; it has not left the museum since
items.cardBack: A Sunday Afternoon on the Island of La Grande Jatte

items.prompt: Which 1907 painting of five nude female figures with angular, fragmented forms and faces influenced by African and Iberian sculpture is considered the work that launched Cubism and hangs at MoMA in New York?
items.explanation:
Pablo Picasso, oil on canvas, 1907, Museum of Modern Art (MoMA), New York
Measures 243.9 x 233.7 cm (8 x 7.7 ft) — nearly square and life-sized
The title refers to a street in Barcelona's red-light district (Carrer d'Avinyó); the women are prostitutes
The two right-hand faces are directly influenced by African masks and Iberian sculpture — a radical departure from European tradition
Shocked even Picasso's closest friends when first shown privately; Matisse thought it was a hoax
Not publicly exhibited until 1916 and not widely seen until the 1930s
Considered the most revolutionary painting of the 20th century and the direct precursor to Cubism
Picasso made over 800 preparatory sketches for the composition

items.prompt: Which Japanese woodblock print from the series *Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji* shows enormous waves threatening boats off the coast, uses imported Prussian blue pigment, and is one of the most reproduced images in art history?
items.explanation:
Katsushika Hokusai, woodblock print (nishiki-e), c. 1831, various collections worldwide
Part of the series *Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji* (Fugaku sanjūrokkei) — Mount Fuji appears small in the background
Measures only 25.7 x 37.9 cm (10.1 x 14.9 in) — a standard ōban size print
The blue color comes from imported Prussian blue (Berlin blue), a synthetic pigment new to Japanese printmaking
Hokusai was about 70 years old when he created this print — he called himself 'the old man mad about drawing'
As a woodblock print, it was produced in multiple impressions; surviving examples exist in museums worldwide
Heavily influenced European Impressionists, especially Monet and Degas, during the Japonisme craze of the 1860s–80s
The wave's claw-like spray and dramatic composition have made it one of the most recognized images in world art

items.prompt: Which ancient Greek marble statue of a goddess, discovered on an Aegean island in 1820 by a farmer, is missing both arms and has been displayed at the Louvre since 1821?
items.explanation:
Unknown sculptor, marble, c. 150–125 BC, Louvre, Paris
Discovered in 1820 on the island of Milos (Melos) in the Aegean Sea by a Greek farmer named Yorgos Kentrotas
Stands 203 cm (6 ft 8 in) tall; carved from Parian marble in two blocks joined at the hips
The arms were already missing at the time of discovery; their original position remains debated — she may have held an apple, a shield, or been spinning thread
Acquired by the French ambassador to the Ottoman Empire and presented to King Louis XVIII, who donated it to the Louvre in 1821
Dated to the Hellenistic period; once attributed to Praxiteles but now considered to be by Alexandros of Antioch based on an inscription on the original plinth (now lost)
Became a symbol of classical Greek beauty and one of the Louvre's three most famous works, alongside the Mona Lisa and Winged Victory
Despite its fame, it was relatively obscure until France promoted it as a replacement for the Medici Venus, which was returned to Italy in 1815

items.prompt: Which headless marble statue of the Greek goddess Nike, dating to c. 190 BC, stands atop a ship's prow at the top of the Daru staircase in the Louvre and is considered one of the greatest surviving Hellenistic sculptures?
items.explanation:
Unknown sculptor, Parian marble, c. 190 BC, Louvre, Paris
Discovered in 1863 by French consul Charles Champoiseau on the island of Samothrace in the northern Aegean
Stands 2.75 m (9 ft) tall on a marble ship's prow; the total monument is 5.57 m (18.3 ft) with the base
Depicts Nike, the Greek goddess of victory, alighting on the bow of a warship with wings spread wide
The head and both arms are missing; the right hand was found in 1950 and is displayed in a case nearby
Placed at the top of the monumental Daru staircase in the Louvre's Denon wing since 1884 — one of the most dramatic museum installations in the world
Wet drapery technique creates the illusion of wind pressing fabric against the body, revealing the figure beneath
May have been created to commemorate a naval victory, possibly by the Rhodians; the exact occasion is debated

items.prompt: Which marble sculpture of the Virgin Mary cradling the body of Christ, completed in 1499, is the only work its creator ever signed — he chiseled his name across the sash of the Madonna after hearing it attributed to another artist?
items.explanation:
Michelangelo, Carrara marble, 1498–1499, St. Peter's Basilica, Vatican City
Commissioned by French Cardinal Jean de Bilhères for his funeral monument; Michelangelo was only 23–24 when he carved it
The only work Michelangelo ever signed — he chiseled 'MICHAELA[N]GELVS BONAROTVS FLORENT[INVS] FACIEBAT' across Mary's sash
Mary is depicted as unusually young, almost the same age as Christ — Michelangelo said the Virgin's purity preserved her youth
Measures 174 x 195 cm (5.7 x 6.4 ft); carved from a single block of marble
Attacked in 1972 by László Tóth with a geologist's hammer, damaging Mary's nose, left arm, and eyelid; restored and now displayed behind bulletproof glass
The triangular composition and serene expression became deeply influential on Renaissance sculpture
Vasari called it 'a miracle that a formless block of stone could ever have been reduced to a perfection that nature is scarcely able to create in the flesh'

items.prompt: Which marble sculpture of two lovers in an embrace, originally titled *Francesca da Rimini* after a character from Dante's *Inferno*, was first modeled in 1882 and its large marble version completed in 1898 by a French sculptor?
items.explanation:
Auguste Rodin, marble (modeled 1882, large marble completed 1888–1898), Musée Rodin, Paris
Originally part of *The Gates of Hell*, depicting the tragic lovers Paolo and Francesca from Dante's *Inferno* (Canto V)
Removed from the Gates because Rodin felt the happy embrace was too different in mood from the rest of the tormented figures
The large marble version was commissioned by the French state in 1888; publicly exhibited at the Salon in 1898
The lovers' lips do not actually touch — they are caught in the moment just before the kiss
Considered scandalous when first shown; a bronze cast sent to the 1904 St. Louis World's Fair was kept in a restricted room
Multiple marble and bronze casts exist; a large marble is also in the Tate Gallery, London
Not to be confused with Klimt's 1907–1908 painting of the same name

items.prompt: Which 1942 painting of four figures in a brightly lit all-night diner on an empty city street, often seen as an image of urban isolation, hangs in the Art Institute of Chicago and was completed just weeks after Pearl Harbor?
items.explanation:
Edward Hopper, oil on canvas, 1942, Art Institute of Chicago
Measures 84.1 x 152.4 cm (33.1 x 60 in); completed on January 21, 1942, weeks after the attack on Pearl Harbor
Depicts three customers and a server in a brightly fluorescent-lit diner at night on an otherwise deserted street corner
The diner has no visible entrance — the figures appear sealed inside, reinforcing themes of urban alienation
Hopper's wife, Josephine, modeled for the red-haired woman; she also kept meticulous records of all his paintings
The setting is thought to be inspired by a real diner in Greenwich Village, Manhattan, now demolished
One of the most reproduced and parodied paintings in American art — numerous versions substitute pop-culture characters
Hopper said 'unconsciously, probably, I was painting the loneliness of a large city'

items.prompt: Which large Romantic painting in the Louvre shows a bare-breasted female figure holding the French tricolour atop a barricade strewn with fallen fighters, commemorating the July Revolution of 1830?
items.explanation:
Eugène Delacroix, oil on canvas, 1830, Louvre, Paris
Measures 260 x 325 cm (8.5 x 10.7 ft); painted in the autumn of 1830, months after the July Revolution that overthrew King Charles X
The central figure is Marianne, a personification of Liberty, wearing a Phrygian cap and holding the French tricolour
Delacroix included himself in the painting — he is believed to be the man in the top hat holding a rifle at left
The boy brandishing pistols at right may have inspired Victor Hugo's character Gavroche in *Les Misérables* (1862)
First exhibited at the 1831 Salon; the French state purchased it but considered it too politically inflammatory and hid it from public view for years
The composition influenced the design of the Statue of Liberty, a gift from France to the United States in 1886
Appeared on the former French 100-franc banknote (1978–1995)
